questions.specification: Case Study: TransportFleet Inc. has a Vehicle class with a startEngine() method that performs basic engine startup. Their Car and Motorcycle subclasses need different starting procedures: cars require fuel pump priming and automatic transmission checks, while motorcycles need kickstand safety checks.

parts.content: Define what is meant by "method overriding" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
Providing a new implementation of a method in a subclass that already exists in the parent class  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Allows subclasses to customize inherited behaviour while maintaining the same interface  <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define the Subclass Relationship
steps.content: Method overriding is a feature in Object-Oriented Programming where a **subclass** (or child class) provides a specific implementation for a method that is already defined in its **superclass** (or parent class). This is a core part of **inheritance**, which you can find in **Topic B3.2.1** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Explain Implementation Replacement
steps.content: The conceptual goal of overriding is to **replace the implementation** of the parent method with a specialized version that is more appropriate for the subclass. In the case study, while the `Vehicle` class has a generic way to `startEngine()`, the `Car` subclass overrides this to include specific steps like "fuel pump priming."

highlights.text: Vehicle class with a startEngine() method

steps.title: Maintain the Method Signature
steps.content: A key requirement for overriding is that the subclass must use the **same method signature** (the same name, return type, and parameters) as the parent. This ensures that the "interface" remains consistent, allowing other parts of the program to call `startEngine()` on any vehicle without needing to know exactly which type of vehicle it is.

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on an IB exam, your definition should include two parts:
1.  **Replacement:** A subclass providing its own version of a method that already exists in the parent class.
2.  **Purpose:** This allows the subclass to customize behavior while keeping the same method name (interface).

This concept is explored further in **Topic B3.2.2: Method Overriding**.

steps.title: Identify the base relationship
steps.content: In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), inheritance allows a subclass to take on the properties and methods of a parent class. Here, the `Car` class inherits the `startEngine()` method from the `Vehicle` class. However, the basic startup logic in the parent class is not sufficient for a car's specific needs.

highlights.text: Vehicle class with a startEngine() method

steps.title: Define method overriding
steps.content:
**Method overriding** is the process where a subclass (like `Car`) provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its parent class (`Vehicle`). 

This earns the first mark by showing that the subclass replaces the inherited logic with its own code while keeping the same method name and parameters.

steps.title: Apply to the Car class
steps.content:
In the TransportFleet Inc. scenario, the `Car` class implements its own version of `startEngine()`. Inside this overridden method, the code would perform the generic engine startup *plus* the specific requirements mentioned: priming the fuel pump and checking the automatic transmission. 

By doing this, the `Car` class effectively "hides" the generic `Vehicle` version and ensures its unique safety checks are performed every time a car is started.

highlights.text: cars require fuel pump priming and automatic transmission checks

steps.title: Explain the benefit
steps.content:
The second mark is awarded for explaining that overriding allows subclasses to **customize inherited behavior** while maintaining the **same interface**. 

In this case study, a programmer can call `.startEngine()` on any object in the fleet without needing to know if it's a car or a motorcycle; the system will automatically run the correct, overridden version of the method for that specific type of vehicle.

parts.content: Describe the rules and requirements for properly overriding methods in inheritance hierarchies.
parts.markscheme:
Rules for method overriding:
Method signature must match exactly (name, parameters, return type)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Access modifier cannot be more restrictive than parent method  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Cannot override static, final, or private methods  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Should use @Override annotation for compile-time checking  <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Match the Method Signature
steps.content:
To successfully override a method like `startEngine()`, the technical signature in the subclass must be an exact match to the one in the superclass. This includes the method **name**, the **parameter list** (the number, type, and order of arguments), and the **return type**.

If any of these differ, the compiler will treat it as a new method (method overloading) rather than an override.

highlights.text: Method signature must match exactly (name, parameters, return type)

steps.title: Check Access Modifier Compatibility
steps.content:
The access modifier of the overriding method cannot be **more restrictive** than the method in the parent class. 

For example, if the `Vehicle` class defines `startEngine()` as `public`, the `Car` subclass cannot try to make it `private` or `protected`. However, it can be more permissive (e.g., overriding a `protected` method with a `public` one).

highlights.text: Access modifier cannot be more restrictive than parent method

steps.title: Identify Non-Overridable Methods
steps.content:
Certain technical keywords prevent overriding entirely:
- **`final` methods**: These are marked specifically to prevent subclasses from changing their behavior.
- **`static` methods**: These belong to the class itself, not an instance, so they are "hidden" rather than overridden.
- **`private` methods**: Since these are not visible to subclasses, they cannot be overridden.

highlights.text: Cannot override static, final, or private methods

steps.title: Apply Compile-Time Checking
steps.content: Technically, it is a requirement of robust OOP design to use the `@Override` annotation. This is a directive to the compiler to verify that a method is actually overriding a superclass method. If you make a typo in the method name while using this annotation, the compiler will generate an error, preventing bugs.

highlights.text: Should use @Override annotation for compile-time checking

steps.title: Understand the Scenario Context
steps.content:
In the **TransportFleet Inc.** case study, we have a base class `Vehicle` with a method `startEngine()`. To allow `Car` and `Motorcycle` to perform their specific checks (like fuel pump priming or kickstand safety), they must **override** this method. This concept is covered in **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**, where we explore how subclasses redefine parent behaviors.

To earn full marks, we need to describe four specific rules for how this works in Java or similar OOP languages.

steps.title: Rule 1: Exact Signature Match
steps.content:
For `Car` to successfully override the behavior, its method must have the exact same **signature** as the one in `Vehicle`. This includes the method name, the parameter list, and the return type.

If `Vehicle` defines:
$$public \space void \space startEngine()$$
Then `Car` must also define:
$$public \space void \space startEngine()$$

If you changed it to `startEngine(int fuel)`, it would be considered **overloading**, not overriding.

highlights.text: Method signature must match exactly (name, parameters, return type)

steps.title: Rule 2: Access Modifier Visibility
steps.content:
The "visibility" of the method in the subclass cannot be more restrictive than in the parent class. 

In our fleet, if `Vehicle` defines `startEngine()` as `public`, the `Motorcycle` class **must** also keep it `public`. It cannot be changed to `private` or `protected` because that would "hide" a method that the parent class promised would be available to everyone.

highlights.text: Access modifier cannot be more restrictive than parent method

steps.title: Rule 3: Non-Overridable Methods
steps.content:
Not every method can be overridden. If the `TransportFleet` developers wanted to prevent subclasses from changing the startup sequence, they could mark the method as `final`. 

Specifically, you cannot override:
1. **final** methods (restricted from change).
2. **static** methods (belong to the class, not the instance).
3. **private** methods (not visible to the subclass at all).

highlights.text: Cannot override static, final, or private methods

steps.title: Rule 4: Use @Override Annotation
steps.content:
While not strictly required for the code to run, it is a requirement for "proper" and safe programming. We should use the `@Override` annotation in the `Car` and `Motorcycle` classes.

$$@Override$$
$$public \space void \space startEngine() \{ ... \}$$

This tells the compiler to double-check that we haven't made a typo (like writing `startEngin()`). If the names don't match perfectly, the compiler will throw an error, saving us from a difficult-to-find bug.

highlights.text: Should use @Override annotation for compile-time checking

parts.content: Using the TransportFleet case study, explain how method overriding would be implemented for the different starting procedures and what precautions should be taken.
parts.markscheme:
Car.startEngine() includes fuel pump activation, transmission safety checks, and ignition sequence  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Motorcycle.startEngine() includes kickstand position check, clutch safety, and electric start sequence  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Precautions: Use @Override annotation, maintain safety protocols, consider calling super.startEngine() for common operations  <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understand Method Overriding
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming, **method overriding** allows a subclass (like `Car` or `Motorcycle`) to provide a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass (`Vehicle`). This is a key part of **Topic B3.2.2: Polymorphism** in the IB HL syllabus.

The goal here is to replace the generic `startEngine()` logic with the specific functional logic required for each vehicle type.

highlights.text: Vehicle class with a startEngine() method

steps.title: Define Car-Specific Logic
steps.content:
For the `Car` subclass, we override `startEngine()` to implement its unique functional sequence. The logic must ensure that the fuel pump is primed and the transmission is in a safe state (usually 'Park' or 'Neutral') before the ignition triggers.

**Logic Sequence for Car:**
1. Activate and prime the fuel pump.
2. Verify the automatic transmission safety check.
3. Execute the standard ignition sequence.

highlights.text: cars require fuel pump priming and automatic transmission checks

steps.title: Define Motorcycle-Specific Logic
steps.content:
The `Motorcycle` subclass requires a different functional logic. Unlike a car, a motorcycle cannot start safely if the kickstand is down while in gear. Therefore, the overridden method must prioritize these mechanical safety checks.

**Logic Sequence for Motorcycle:**
1. Check the kickstand position sensor.
2. Confirm the clutch is engaged or the bike is in neutral.
3. Trigger the electric start sequence.

steps.title: Apply Essential Precautions
steps.content:
When implementing overriding, three precautions are vital for robust code:

1.  **Use the `@Override` annotation**: This tells the compiler to check that the method signature exactly matches the one in `Vehicle`, preventing bugs from typos.
2.  **Maintain Safety Protocols**: Ensure that the specific logic (like the kickstand check) is completed *before* the engine actually turns over.
3.  **Use `super.startEngine()`**: If there is common logic in the base `Vehicle` class (like checking battery levels), the subclass should call the parent method to ensure that global requirements are still met.

$$ \text{Subclass Logic} = \text{super.startEngine()} + \text{Specific Implementation} $$

steps.title: Define Method Overriding Mechanics
steps.content:
In the **Technical OOP Approach**, method overriding occurs when a subclass (like `Car` or `Motorcycle`) provides a specific implementation for a method already defined in its superclass (`Vehicle`). To implement this correctly, the method signature in the subclass—including the name, return type, and parameters—must be **identical** to the one in the parent class.

This is a core part of **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**, which focuses on how polymorphism allows a single method call to behave differently depending on the object type.

highlights.text: Vehicle class with a startEngine() method

steps.title: Implement Car.startEngine()
steps.content:
For the `Car` subclass, we override `startEngine()` to include specific safety and mechanical steps. The implementation should focus on the technical requirements for a car:

$$\begin{aligned} &1. \text{ Activate fuel pump (priming)} \\ &2. \text{ Perform automatic transmission checks} \\ &3. \text{ Execute ignition sequence} \end{aligned}$$

By keeping the signature identical, any part of the program holding a `Vehicle` reference can call `startEngine()`, and if the object is a `Car`, these specific steps will run.

highlights.text: fuel pump priming and automatic transmission checks

steps.title: Implement Motorcycle.startEngine()
steps.content:
Similarly, the `Motorcycle` subclass overrides the method to handle its unique safety protocols. Technically, this ensures that the motorcycle-specific hardware is ready before the engine turns over:

$$\begin{aligned} &1. \text{ Check kickstand position (safety sensor)} \\ &2. \text{ Verify clutch engagement} \\ &3. \text{ Execute electric start sequence} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply Technical Precautions
steps.content:
When overriding, several formal precautions ensure code stability and safety:

1.  **Use the `@Override` Annotation**: This tells the compiler to check that the method actually exists in the parent class. If you make a typo in the name (e.g., `startEngin()`), the compiler will throw an error rather than creating a new, unrelated method.
2.  **The `super` Keyword**: If the `Vehicle` class has general startup logic (like logging the start time), the subclasses should call `super.startEngine()` within their own implementation. This **extends** parent functionality rather than completely replacing it.
3.  **Access Modifiers**: Ensure the subclass method does not have a more restrictive access level than the parent (e.g., if the parent is `public`, the override cannot be `private`).

questions.specification: Case Study: DesignStudio Pro is creating a drawing application where users can create different shapes (`Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle`) that all need to calculate area and perimeter, but each shape uses different mathematical formulas for these calculations. The application needs to handle these objects through common methods such as `calculateArea()` and `calculatePerimeter()`.

parts.content: State what is meant by "polymorphism" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
The ability of objects of different types to respond to the same method call/message in their own specific way. <marks>A1</marks>

"One interface, multiple implementations" — where the same method name is associated with different behaviours depending on the object. <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Define the core concept
steps.content:
In object-oriented programming, **polymorphism** (which literally means "many forms") refers to the ability of objects belonging to different classes to respond to the **same method call or message** in their own specific way.

This is a fundamental pillar of OOP that allows a single interface to be used for a general category of actions.

highlights.text: The ability of objects of different types to respond to the same method call/message in their own specific way.

steps.title: One interface, multiple implementations
steps.content:
A key way to describe polymorphism is as **"one interface, multiple implementations."** 

This means that while the method name (the interface) remains the same, the actual code that runs (the implementation) is different and is determined by the specific type of the object at runtime.

highlights.text: "One interface, multiple implementations" — where the same method name is associated with different behaviours depending on the object.

steps.title: Relate to the case study
steps.content:
In the context of the DesignStudio Pro application, polymorphism allows the program to call `$calculateArea()$` on any shape object. 

Even though the instruction is the same, a `Circle` object will use the formula $A = \pi r^2$ while a `Rectangle` object will use $A = l \times w$. The application doesn't need to know the specific shape to trigger the calculation; it just needs to know that the object "knows" how to calculate its area.

steps.title: Define the general concept
steps.content:
Polymorphism is a core pillar of Object-Oriented Programming (covered in **Topic B3: Object-oriented programming**). At its heart, it is the ability of different types of objects to respond to the same method call in their own unique way.

In the context of the DesignStudio Pro case study, this means that even though a `Circle` and a `Rectangle` are different shapes, the application can treat them both as "Shapes" and ask them to calculate their area using the exact same command.

steps.title: One Interface
steps.content:
Using the **Interface-Based Definition**, we describe polymorphism as having **"one interface"**. 

The "interface" here refers to the method name and signature that is shared across different classes. For example, the method `calculateArea()` serves as a common interface for all shape objects. The programmer doesn't need to know if they are dealing with a circle or a square; they just call that specific method name.

steps.title: Multiple Implementations
steps.content:
The second half of the definition is **"multiple implementations"**. 

While the method name is the same, the actual code (the "behavior") inside that method changes depending on the object's class:
- The `Circle` implementation uses $\pi r^2$.
- The `Rectangle` implementation uses $length \times width$.

Because the same method name is associated with different behaviors depending on the object, we achieve polymorphism.

steps.title: Final Definition for Marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks, your answer should combine these ideas:

1.  **Mark 1:** State that it is the ability of objects of different types to respond to the same method call/message in their own specific way.
2.  **Mark 2:** Use the phrasing **"one interface, multiple implementations"**, explaining that the same method name triggers different behaviors depending on the object type.

steps.title: Identify the common interface
steps.content:
In the DesignStudio Pro case study, we see different objects like circles and rectangles. Even though they are different shapes, the application wants to treat them similarly by using the same method names: `calculateArea()` and `calculatePerimeter()`.

This core idea—having one consistent way to interact with different types of objects—is the foundation of **polymorphism**.

steps.title: Define through different responses
steps.content:
The first mark for this definition comes from explaining that **objects of different types respond to the same method call in their own specific way**.

In our scenario, if the program sends the message `calculateArea()` to a `Circle` object, it responds using the logic for a circle. If it sends that exact same message to a `Rectangle`, the rectangle responds using its own specific logic. Both "hear" the same command but act differently.

steps.title: Explain multiple implementations
steps.content:
The second mark involves the concept of "one interface, multiple implementations." This means the **same method name is associated with different behaviors** depending on the object being used.

For example, the internal math (implementation) is completely different:

- For a `Circle`, the calculation is: 
$$\text{Area} = \pi r^2$$
- For a `Rectangle`, the calculation is: 
$$\text{Area} = \text{length} \times \text{width}$$

Polymorphism allows the drawing application to call the same method name without needing to know which formula is being used inside the object.

steps.title: Final Definition Summary
steps.content:
To get full marks on an IB exam, you should state that polymorphism is:
1. The ability of different types of objects to respond to the same method call in their own specific way.
2. A system of "one interface, multiple implementations" where the behavior depends on the object's class.

This concept is a key part of **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**.

parts.content: Compare compile-time polymorphism (method overloading) with runtime polymorphism (method overriding), explaining when each occurs.
parts.markscheme:
**Compile-time polymorphism (Overloading):**
Resolved at compile time based on the method signature (the number or types of parameters). <marks>A1</marks>

The compiler determines which specific version of a method to call before the program executes. <marks>A1</marks>

**Runtime polymorphism (Overriding):**
Resolved at runtime based on the actual type of the object instance being referenced (dynamic binding). <marks>A1</marks>

Occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class, allowing the program to decide the implementation during execution. <marks>A1</marks>

[4 marks total]

steps.title: Identify the two types
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), polymorphism allows one interface to be used for a general class of actions. To answer this question, we must distinguish between how the computer handles **method overloading** versus **method overriding**.

This concept is covered in **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes (HL only)**. If you are unfamiliar with inheritance, you may want to review that section first.

highlights.text: Compare compile-time polymorphism (method overloading) with runtime polymorphism (method overriding)

steps.title: Compile-time: Method Overloading
steps.content:
Compile-time polymorphism, also known as **method overloading**, occurs when multiple methods in the same class have the same name but different **method signatures** (the number, type, or order of parameters).

Key characteristics:
- **Timing**: It is resolved by the compiler **before** the program executes.
- **Mechanism**: The compiler looks at the arguments passed during the call to determine exactly which version of the method to link to.

steps.title: Runtime: Method Overriding
steps.content:
Runtime polymorphism, or **method overriding**, occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation for a method that is already defined in its parent class. In the case study, this happens when `Circle` or `Rectangle` provides its own unique logic for `calculateArea()`.

Key characteristics:
- **Timing**: It is resolved at **runtime** (during execution).
- **Mechanism**: It uses **dynamic binding**, where the program checks the actual type of the object instance being referenced (e.g., is it actually a `Circle`?) to decide which implementation to run.

steps.title: Summary of differences
steps.content:
To secure all 4 marks, ensure you clearly contrast the resolution time and the underlying requirement for each:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Feature} & \textbf{Overloading (Compile-time)} & \textbf{Overriding (Runtime)} \\ \hline \text{Resolution Time} & \text{During compilation} & \text{During execution} \\ \hline \text{Based on...} & \text{Method signature (parameters)} & \text{Actual object type} \\ \hline \text{Requirement} & \text{Same class / Different params} & \text{Inheritance / Same signature} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

According to the markscheme, the first two marks are awarded for explaining the timing and signature basis of overloading, while the final two marks are awarded for explaining dynamic binding and the subclass implementation in overriding.

steps.title: Understand the Scenario
steps.content:
In our **DesignStudio Pro** application, we are dealing with a hierarchy of shapes. To understand polymorphism, we look at how the computer chooses between different methods with the same name. 

This concept is part of **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes (HL only)**. Specifically, we are looking at how `Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle` interact with their parent class.

steps.title: Compile-time Polymorphism (Overloading)
steps.content:
Compile-time polymorphism, or **method overloading**, occurs when you have multiple methods in the *same class* with the same name but different **method signatures** (different number or types of parameters).

In our case study, imagine a `Circle` class with two ways to draw it:
1. `draw()` — uses a default color.
2. `draw(String color)` — uses a specific color.

The compiler determines which specific version to call **before the program executes** based on the arguments you provide. If you write `myCircle.draw("Red")`, the compiler knows exactly which code block to link to right then and there.

highlights.text: Resolved at compile time based on the method signature (the number or types of parameters).
The compiler determines which specific version of a method to call before the program executes.

steps.title: Runtime Polymorphism (Overriding)
steps.content:
Runtime polymorphism, or **method overriding**, happens when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class. 

In the DesignStudio scenario, `Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle` all have their own version of `calculateArea()`. Because each uses a different formula, they **override** the base method in the `Shape` class.

$$\text{Area}_{Circle} = \pi r^2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Area}_{Rectangle} = w \times h$$

highlights.text: Occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class, allowing the program to decide the implementation during execution.

steps.title: Dynamic Binding in Action
steps.content:
The key difference is *when* the decision is made. In runtime polymorphism, the decision is delayed until the program is actually running (**dynamic binding**).

If you have a list of generic `Shape` objects, the program doesn't know if the next item is a `Circle` or a `Triangle` until it reaches it. It checks the **actual type of the object instance** during execution to ensure the correct math formula is applied for that specific shape.

highlights.text: Resolved at runtime based on the actual type of the object instance being referenced (dynamic binding).

parts.content: Using the DesignStudio case study, describe how polymorphism would allow treating different shapes uniformly while maintaining specific area calculations.
parts.markscheme:
A `Shape` base class (or interface) defines abstract methods such as `calculateArea()` and `calculatePerimeter()`. <marks>A1</marks>

Each specific subclass (`Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle`) overrides these methods with its own geometry-specific implementation. <marks>A1</marks>

Polymorphism allows the application to store all shape objects in a single collection (e.g., an array of `Shape`) and call `calculateArea()` on each element without needing to know its specific subclass, ensuring the correct formula is always applied. <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks total]

steps.title: Define the abstract superclass
steps.content: To implement polymorphism using an inheritance-based approach, we first define an abstract superclass called `Shape`. This class serves as a blueprint that declares the common behavior for all shapes. In this case, we define method signatures for `calculateArea()` and `calculatePerimeter()` without providing their actual math logic yet.

highlights.text: A Shape base class (or interface) defines abstract methods such as calculateArea() and calculatePerimeter().

steps.title: Extend and override methods
steps.content:
Next, specific subclasses like `Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle` use the `extends` keyword to inherit from the `Shape` class. Each of these subclasses **overrides** the abstract methods to provide its specific implementation. 

For example:
- The `Circle` class implements `calculateArea()` using $A = \pi r^2$
- The `Rectangle` class implements it using $A = length \times width$

highlights.text: Each specific subclass (Circle, Rectangle, Triangle) overrides these methods with its own geometry-specific implementation.

steps.title: Treat shapes uniformly
steps.content:
Because of inheritance, polymorphism allows us to treat all these different objects as if they were just generic `Shape` objects. We can store a mix of circles, rectangles, and triangles in a single collection, such as an array of type `Shape[]`. 

When the application loops through this collection and calls `.calculateArea()`, the computer automatically determines which specific subclass it is dealing with and executes the correct formula for that shape.

highlights.text: Polymorphism allows the application to store all shape objects in a single collection (e.g., an array of Shape) and call calculateArea() on each element without needing to know its specific subclass, ensuring the correct formula is always applied.

steps.title: Define the Interface Contract
steps.content: To treat different shapes uniformly, we first define a **Shape interface**. This acts as a 'contract' that specifies the methods every shape must have, such as `calculateArea()` and `calculatePerimeter()`. By doing this, we create a common type that the application can recognize, regardless of whether the specific shape is a circle or a rectangle.

steps.title: Implement Specific logic
steps.content: Each specific class—`Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle`—must **implement** the Shape interface. This means they each provide their own unique version of the mathematical formulas (method overriding). For example, the `Circle` class will use $\pi r^2$ inside its `calculateArea()` method, while the `Rectangle` class will use $length \times width$.

steps.title: Apply Polymorphism in Practice
steps.content: Polymorphism allows the application to store all these different objects in a single collection, such as an `Array` or `List` of type `Shape`. When the application iterates through this list and calls `.calculateArea()`, the computer automatically determines which specific version to run based on the object's actual class. This allows for 'uniform' handling while ensuring the 'specific' correct area calculation is performed.

steps.title: Establish a common base
steps.content: To treat different shapes uniformly, we first need a common point of reference. We define a **Shape** base class (or interface) that includes abstract methods like `calculateArea()` and `calculatePerimeter()`. This creates a "contract" that any specific shape must follow.

steps.title: Implement specific behaviors
steps.content: Each specific subclass, such as `Circle`, `Rectangle`, and `Triangle`, **overrides** these abstract methods. This allows each class to provide its own unique mathematical formula for area and perimeter while keeping the method name exactly the same.

steps.title: Use a unified collection
steps.content: By using polymorphism, the application can store all these different objects in a single collection, such as an array of type `Shape[]`. When we iterate through this list and call `calculateArea()`, **dynamic binding** occurs at runtime. The computer automatically detects whether the object is a Circle or a Rectangle and executes the correct formula, allowing the application to process many different shapes with one simple loop.

questions.specification: Case Study: AeronauticsDesign Corp wants to create a $\text{FlyingCar}$ class that needs characteristics from both their existing $\text{Car}$ class (wheels, steering, land navigation) and $\text{Aircraft}$ class (wings, flight controls, air navigation), but their programming language doesn't support multiple inheritance.

parts.content: Define "multiple inheritance" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- Define multiple inheritance as a feature where a class can inherit from more than one parent class. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify that this allows for combining functionality from multiple base classes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the diamond problem that can occur with multiple inheritance and describe how different programming languages address this issue.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that the diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two parent classes that share a common base class. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that this creates ambiguity about which parent's implementation of a method should be used by the child class. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that languages like Java and C# address this by using interfaces instead of multiple inheritance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that languages like Python and C++ provide resolution mechanisms such as Method Resolution Order (MRO). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the AeronauticsDesign case study, analyse the challenges this creates and suggest design solutions that avoid multiple inheritance problems.
parts.markscheme:
- Analyse that challenges include potential method conflicts between $\text{Car}$ and $\text{Aircraft}$, or a diamond problem if both share a common $\text{Vehicle}$ base. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suggest using composition where the $\text{FlyingCar}$ class contains instances of both $\text{Car}$ and $\text{Aircraft}$ objects. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suggest using interfaces for common behaviours (such as $\text{Drivable}$ and $\text{Flyable}$) with single inheritance for implementation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Case Study: MotorVehicles Inc. has a `Vehicle` class with basic initialization. Their `Motorcycle` class needs to call the `Vehicle` constructor to set `make` and `model`, then add motorcycle-specific attributes like `engineDisplacement` and `handlebarType`.

parts.content: State what is meant by the `super` keyword in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- States that the `super` keyword refers to the immediate parent class of the current class. <marks>A1</marks>

- Identifies that it allows access to parent class members, such as constructors, methods, and variables. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the reference
steps.content: In object-oriented programming, the `super` keyword serves as a reference variable that points directly to the **immediate parent class** (also known as the superclass) of the current class.

steps.title: Explain functional access
steps.content:
The primary purpose of `super` is to allow a subclass to access or invoke members of its parent class. This includes:

*   **Constructors:** Calling the parent's initialization logic (as seen in the `Motorcycle` and `Vehicle` example).
*   **Methods:** Invoking a version of a method defined in the parent class.
*   **Variables:** Accessing fields defined in the parent class.

highlights.text: allows access to parent class members, such as constructors, methods, and variables

steps.title: Relate to the hierarchy
steps.content: In the context of the **inheritance hierarchy** (covered in **Topic B3.2**), `super` ensures that the subclass can leverage existing code from the level directly above it, promoting code reusability and proper object initialization.

steps.title: Identify the Parent Reference
steps.content: In object-oriented programming, the `super` keyword is used within a subclass (child class) to refer directly to its **immediate parent class**. This establishes a link between the child and the base class from which it inherits properties and behaviors.

steps.title: Functional Usage: Invoking Constructors
steps.content:
One of the most common practical functions of `super` is to **invoke the parent class constructor**. 

In our case study, the `Motorcycle` class uses `super` to pass the `make` and `model` values up to the `Vehicle` constructor. This ensures that the basic initialization logic defined in the parent class is executed before the motorcycle adds its own specific attributes like `engineDisplacement`.

highlights.text: Motorcycle class needs to call the Vehicle constructor

steps.title: Functional Usage: Accessing Members
steps.content:
Beyond constructors, `super` provides a way to **access parent class members**, such as methods and variables. 

This is particularly useful when a child class has **overridden** a method but still needs to execute the original version from the parent. By using `super.methodName()`, the program explicitly bypasses the child's implementation to use the parent's logic.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks in an IB exam for this definition, you must mention both parts:
1.  **Reference:** It refers to the immediate parent class ($1$ mark).
2.  **Function:** It allows access to parent members like constructors or overridden methods ($1$ mark).

This concept is a core part of **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**.

steps.title: Identify the parent-child relationship
steps.content: To understand the `super` keyword, we first look at the relationship between the classes in the **MotorVehicles** scenario. Here, `Vehicle` is the **parent class** (or superclass), and `Motorcycle` is the **child class** (or subclass) that inherits from it.

steps.title: Reference the immediate parent
steps.content: In object-oriented programming, the `super` keyword is used within a child class (like `Motorcycle`) to refer directly to its **immediate parent class** (in this case, `Vehicle`). This tells the program to look at the logic defined in the class one level higher in the hierarchy.

steps.title: Access parent class members
steps.content:
The keyword allows the `Motorcycle` class to access **parent class members**, such as its constructor or methods. 

In our scenario, `Motorcycle` uses `super` to call the `Vehicle` constructor. This ensures that the code for setting `make` and `model` is reused from the parent class, rather than being rewritten inside the `Motorcycle` class.

highlights.text: call the Vehicle constructor to set make and model

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content:
To summarize for your exam marks:
1. The `super` keyword refers to the **immediate parent class** of the current class.
2. It allows the child class to **access members** (like constructors, methods, or variables) of that parent class.

parts.content: Describe the different ways the `super` keyword can be used in inheritance relationships and explain why it is important.
parts.markscheme:
- Describes the use of `super()` to invoke the parent class constructor. <marks>A1</marks>

- Describes the use of `super.methodName()` to invoke a specific implementation of a method defined in the parent class. <marks>A1</marks>

- Describes the use of `super.variableName` to access public or protected variables belonging to the parent class. <marks>A1</marks>

- Explains that `super` is important for promoting code reuse and ensuring the proper initialization of the inheritance chain. <marks>R1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Constructor Invocation
steps.content:
The first syntactic use of `super` is to invoke the parent class constructor using the syntax `super()`. 

In the provided case study, the `Motorcycle` class would use this in its own constructor to pass the `make` and `model` values up to the `Vehicle` class. This ensures that the attributes inherited from the parent are correctly initialized before the child class adds its specific attributes like `engineDisplacement`.

$$\text{Example: } super(make, model);$$

highlights.text: call the Vehicle constructor to set make and model

steps.title: Method Implementation Access
steps.content:
The second use is to invoke a specific method defined in the parent class using the syntax `super.methodName()`. 

This is particularly useful in **method overriding**. If the `Motorcycle` class has a version of a method (like `calculateTax()`) that is different from the `Vehicle` class, but you still want to execute the original logic from the parent, you call it via `super`. This allows you to extend the parent's behavior rather than completely replacing it.

steps.title: Variable Access
steps.content:
The third syntactic implementation is accessing parent variables using `super.variableName`. 

This allows a child class to explicitly access public or protected fields in the parent class. This is often used if the child class has a variable with the same name as one in the parent (known as variable shadowing), ensuring the program knows exactly which version of the data you are referring to.

steps.title: Importance in OOP
steps.content:
The `super` keyword is essential for maintaining the **inheritance chain**. 

It promotes **code reusability** because developers don't have to rewrite the initialization logic or method code already present in the parent class. Furthermore, it ensures that objects are initialized in the correct order (parent first, then child), which prevents errors where child methods might try to access uninitialized parent data.

steps.title: Invoking the Parent Constructor
steps.content:
The most common use of the `super` keyword is to call the constructor of the parent class. In the MotorVehicles Inc. scenario, the `Motorcycle` class is a subclass of `Vehicle`. Instead of manually re-assigning the `make` and `model` attributes, the `Motorcycle` constructor uses `super(make, model)` to pass these values up to the `Vehicle` constructor.

This ensures that the shared attributes are initialized exactly how the parent class intended, maintaining data integrity from the start.

steps.title: Accessing Parent Methods
steps.content:
The `super` keyword can also be used to call a specific method defined in the parent class using the syntax `super.methodName()`. 

For example, if the `Vehicle` class has a method called `displayInfo()`, the `Motorcycle` class might override this method to add details like `engineDisplacement`. Within its own version of the method, it can call `super.displayInfo()` to print the basic make and model first, and then add its specific details afterwards. This prevents code duplication.

steps.title: Accessing Parent Variables
steps.content:
In some cases, `super` is used to access public or protected variables belonging to the parent class using `super.variableName`. 

This is particularly useful if a subclass has a variable with the same name as one in the parent class (known as variable shadowing). By using `super`, the programmer explicitly tells the system to use the version of the variable stored in the parent `Vehicle` class rather than the local one in `Motorcycle`.

steps.title: Importance of 'super'
steps.content:
The `super` keyword is vital for two main reasons:
1. **Code Reuse:** It allows subclasses like `Motorcycle` to leverage existing logic in the `Vehicle` class, which reduces the amount of code that needs to be written and maintained.
2. **Initialization Chain:** It ensures that every class in an inheritance hierarchy is properly set up. By calling the parent constructor, we guarantee that the "foundation" of the object is built before we add specific features like `handlebarType`.

steps.title: Establish the Inheritance Hierarchy
steps.content: In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), inheritance creates a parent-child relationship. The `super` keyword acts as the essential bridge or link that allows the child class to "look up" to its parent. Without `super`, a child class would be isolated from the logic already defined in its parent, defeating the purpose of the hierarchy.

steps.title: Invoking the Parent Constructor
steps.content:
The most common way to activate this link is through `super()`. This is used within the child class constructor to call the parent class's constructor. 

In our case study, the `Motorcycle` class uses this to ensure that the `make` and `model` (which belong to the `Vehicle` class) are correctly initialized before adding its own unique attributes like `engineDisplacement`.

highlights.text: Motorcycle class needs to call the Vehicle constructor

steps.title: Accessing Parent Methods
steps.content:
A child class can also use the link to trigger specific behaviors from the parent using the syntax:

$$super.methodName()$$

This is particularly useful during **method overriding**. If the `Motorcycle` class overrides a `displayInfo()` method but still wants to execute the original logic from `Vehicle`, it can call the parent version first and then add its own motorcycle-specific details.

steps.title: Accessing Parent Variables
steps.content:
Similarly, the hierarchy allows the child to directly access data held by the parent using:

$$super.variableName$$

This provides access to `public` or `protected` variables defined in the parent class. While encapsulation usually favors using methods, this syntax is a valid way to reference specific fields belonging to the parent in the inheritance chain.

steps.title: The Importance of Super
steps.content:
The `super` keyword is critical for two main reasons:
1.  **Code Reuse:** It prevents us from rewriting code. We don't need to redefine how `make` and `model` are stored because `Vehicle` already does it.
2.  **Inheritance Integrity:** It ensures the **initialization chain** is maintained. By calling the parent constructor, we guarantee that the object is fully and correctly built from the top of the hierarchy down to the child.

parts.content: Using the MotorVehicles case study, explain how `super` would be used in the `Motorcycle` constructor and why this approach is beneficial.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies that the `Motorcycle` constructor calls `super(make, model)` to initialize the attributes inherited from the `Vehicle` class. <marks>A1</marks>

- Describes how motorcycle-specific attributes, such as `engineDisplacement` and `handlebarType`, are assigned after the `super` call. <marks>A1</marks>

- Explains that this approach is beneficial as it maintains the integrity of the object hierarchy and avoids duplicating initialization logic already present in the parent class. <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Initialize the Parent Class
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming, when a class inherits from another, the subclass constructor must first call the parent constructor. In this case, the `Motorcycle` constructor begins by calling `super(make, model)`.

This instruction passes the common data up to the `Vehicle` class so it can initialize the inherited attributes. In many languages, this must be the very first statement in the constructor to ensure the base object is built before adding specific details.

steps.title: Assign Specific Attributes
steps.content:
Once the `super()` call is complete and the parent attributes are set, the procedural flow returns to the `Motorcycle` constructor. Now, the motorcycle-specific attributes are assigned:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{this.engineDisplacement} &= \text{engineDisplacement} \\ \text{this.handlebarType} &= \text{handlebarType} \end{aligned}$$

This ensures that the general "Vehicle" identity is established before the specific "Motorcycle" features are added.

steps.title: Identify Key Benefits
steps.content:
Using this approach provides two main advantages:

1.  **Code Reusability**: It avoids duplicating the initialization logic for `make` and `model` that already exists in the `Vehicle` class.
2.  **Object Integrity**: It maintains a clear hierarchy, ensuring that the parent state is valid and fully formed before the subclass attempts to modify the object. This follows the principles of **Inheritance** covered in **Topic B3.2**.

steps.title: Initialize inherited attributes
steps.content:
In Object-Oriented Programming, when a subclass like `Motorcycle` extends a parent class like `Vehicle`, it must first ensure the parent's data is set up. Inside the `Motorcycle` constructor, the first action is to call `super(make, model)`. 

This instruction passes the required data up to the `Vehicle` constructor, allowing it to initialize the attributes it is responsible for.

highlights.text: call the Vehicle constructor to set make and model

steps.title: Assign subclass-specific attributes
steps.content:
Once the parent class has finished its initialization, the `Motorcycle` constructor then handles the attributes that are unique to its own class. 

At this stage, the program assigns values to `this.engineDisplacement` and `this.handlebarType`. This ensures that the object is fully formed with both its general vehicle data and its specific motorcycle features.

steps.title: Analyze the design benefits
steps.content:
Using `super` follows key design principles in software engineering:

1.  **Code Reusability**: We don't have to rewrite the code that sets the `make` and `model`. We simply reuse what already exists in the `Vehicle` class.
2.  **Maintaining Hierarchy**: It preserves the integrity of the object hierarchy. The `Motorcycle` correctly acknowledges it "is a" `Vehicle` by letting the parent manage its own state.
3.  **Avoiding Duplication**: If the logic for initializing a `Vehicle` changes (e.g., adding a validation check), we only need to change it in one place rather than in every subclass.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must include the following points:

*   **A1**: State that `super(make, model)` is called to initialize inherited attributes.
*   **A1**: Explain that unique attributes like `engineDisplacement` are assigned *after* the `super` call.
*   **R1**: Provide a reasoning based on design principles, such as maintaining the object hierarchy or avoiding code duplication.

questions.specification: Case Study: GraphicsEngine Ltd. has a $\texttt{Shape}$ hierarchy where $\texttt{Circle}$ and $\texttt{Rectangle}$ classes both override the $\texttt{draw()}$ method from the base $\texttt{Shape}$ class. When the rendering system calls $\texttt{draw()}$ on a $\texttt{Shape}$ reference that points to a $\texttt{Circle}$ object, it needs to execute $\texttt{Circle}$'s specific drawing code.

parts.content: State what is meant by "method resolution" in polymorphic inheritance hierarchies.
parts.markscheme:
- State that method resolution is the process of determining which method implementation to call when multiple versions exist in an inheritance hierarchy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that it is resolved at runtime based on the actual object type, rather than the reference type, to enable polymorphism <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how object-oriented languages enable dynamic method dispatch for overridden methods.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe that each object has a way of identifying its actual (runtime) class/type <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe that when an overridden method is called, the runtime uses the object’s actual class/type to select the correct implementation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe that this selection happens at the moment of invocation (runtime), not at compile time, enabling polymorphism <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe that the chosen implementation is the overridden method belonging to the actual object type (e.g., subclass), not the reference type <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the GraphicsEngine case study, explain the process that occurs when $\texttt{draw()}$ is called on a $\texttt{Shape}$ reference pointing to a $\texttt{Circle}$ object.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that while the reference type is $\texttt{Shape}$, the actual object being pointed to is an instance of $\texttt{Circle}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that at runtime the system determines the object’s actual type ($\texttt{Circle}$) to decide which $\texttt{draw()}$ to run <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that the $\texttt{Circle}$ class’s implementation of $\texttt{draw()}$ is executed instead of the $\texttt{Shape}$ version, demonstrating dynamic dispatch <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Case Study: WildlifePreserve is developing a zoo management system where they need to model different animals. All animals share common attributes (name, age, species, habitat) but have specific behaviours like `Lion.roar()`, `Elephant.trumpet()`, and `Penguin.swim()`.

parts.content: Define what is meant by "inheritance" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** inheritance as a mechanism where a new class (subclass) derives properties and methods from an existing class (superclass) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** that inheritance creates an "is-a" relationship between parent (superclass) and child (subclass) classes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the benefits and potential drawbacks of using inheritance in software design.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that inheritance promotes code reuse, which reduces code duplication and decreases overall development time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that it establishes logical hierarchies that effectively model real-world relationships within the system <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that inheritance can create tight coupling, where changes made to a parent class may break or require modifications in all child classes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that deep inheritance hierarchies can become overly complex, making the system difficult to maintain and understand <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the WildlifePreserve case study, analyse how inheritance could be used to model different animal types and discuss the advantages of this approach.
parts.markscheme:
- **Describe** the implementation of an `Animal` base class containing common attributes (name, age, species, habitat), with specialized classes like `Lion` or `Elephant` inheriting from it <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that each subclass adds specific behaviours (e.g., `Lion.roar()`, `Elephant.trumpet()`) while sharing common zoo management methods from the superclass <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Analyse/Discuss** an advantage such as ease of extending the system with new species (add a new subclass with minimal changes elsewhere) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Analyse/Discuss** an advantage such as maintaining a consistent interface for managing all animals (e.g., treating all animals as `Animal` for storage/iteration) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Scenario: GameEngine Pro is developing a framework for video games where they need to define common behaviour for all game entities (position, health, collision detection) while ensuring that specific entity types (Player, Enemy, PowerUp) must implement their own update and render methods.

parts.content: State what is meant by "abstract classes" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that abstract classes are classes that cannot be instantiated directly and often contain methods without implementations <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that they serve as base classes or blueprints that define a common interface and provide partial implementation for subclasses <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Define Instantiation Restrictions
steps.content:
From a formal technical perspective, the most defining characteristic of an **abstract class** is that it **cannot be instantiated directly**. 

This means you cannot create an object from it using the `new` keyword. In our GameEngine Pro scenario, you could not create an object of type `GameEntity`; you can only create objects of its concrete subclasses like `Player` or `Enemy`.

highlights.text: abstract classes are classes that cannot be instantiated directly

steps.title: Explain Implementation Gaps
steps.content:
Abstract classes often contain **abstract methods**, which are method signatures (headers) that **lack an implementation** (no code body). 

For example, the `GameEntity` class might define a method signature for `update()`, but because every entity behaves differently, the abstract class leaves the actual code for the subclasses to define. This ensures that any class inheriting from it is technically forced to provide its own specific logic.

steps.title: Identify the Blueprint Role
steps.content:
Technically, an abstract class functions as a **base class or blueprint**. It establishes a **common interface** (a set of methods that all subclasses must have) while potentially providing some shared code (partial implementation), such as managing `position` or `health` variables that all entities use.

This provides a structured hierarchy covered in **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**.

highlights.text: serve as base classes or blueprints that define a common interface and provide partial implementation for subclasses

steps.title: The Blueprint Concept
steps.content:
In object-oriented programming, an **abstract class** acts as a structural blueprint for other classes. Just as a blueprint for a "Game Entity" defines what every entity should have (like health or position) without being a playable character itself, an abstract class defines a template that cannot be turned into an object on its own.

This concept is a key part of **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**, where we explore how classes relate to one another in a hierarchy.

steps.title: Instantiation and Abstract Methods
steps.content:
The first defining characteristic is that an abstract class **cannot be instantiated**. This means you cannot use the `new` keyword to create an object directly from it. 

Additionally, it often contains **abstract methods**—methods that are declared with a name and parameters but have no code (no implementation). In our scenario, `update()` and `render()` are examples of these

steps.title: Defining the Common Interface
steps.content:
The second key role of an abstract class is to serve as a **base class** or **blueprint**. It defines a **common interface** that all subclasses must follow. 

By including abstract methods, the abstract class *enforces* a rule: any non-abstract subclass (like `Player` or `Enemy`) MUST provide its own specific implementation for those methods. This ensures consistency across the entire game engine framework.

steps.title: Identify the base concept
steps.content:
In the context of **GameEngine Pro**, we have a class called `GameEntity`. This class holds data that every object in the game shares, like **position** or **health**. 

However, it wouldn't make sense to create a generic "Entity" object in the game world because the engine wouldn't know how to draw it or how it should move. This brings us to the first key characteristic of an abstract class: **it cannot be instantiated directly**.

steps.title: Define the blueprint role
steps.content:
Abstract classes like `GameEntity` act as a **blueprint** or template for other classes. While they can provide some shared code (like collision detection logic), they also define **abstract methods**—methods like `update()` and `render()` that have no body.

By doing this, the abstract class enforces a **common interface**. It tells the `Player` and `Enemy` classes: "If you want to be a part of this game engine, you MUST provide your own specific implementation for how you update and render."

highlights.text: serve as base classes or blueprints

steps.title: Summarize for the marks
steps.content:
To gain full marks, ensure your definition includes these two points:
1.  **Non-instantiation**: They are classes that cannot be used to create objects directly and often contain methods without code (abstract methods).
2.  **Blueprint/Interface**: They provide a common structure or "contract" that subclasses (like `Player`) must follow to ensure consistency across the program.

This concept is a core part of **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**.

parts.content: Compare abstract classes with concrete classes, explaining when abstract classes are appropriate to use.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that concrete classes can be instantiated to create objects, whereas abstract classes cannot be used to create objects directly <marks>A1</marks>
- **Contrast** the methods, noting that abstract classes can contain abstract methods (without bodies), while concrete classes must have implementations for all methods <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that abstract classes are appropriate when you want to share common code and attributes among multiple subclasses while preventing the creation of the generic parent object <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that they are used to enforce a specific structure (contract) on all inheriting subclasses through abstract method declarations <marks>A1</marks>

[4 marks total]

steps.title: Comparison 1: Instantiation
steps.content:
The first major difference lies in whether you can actually create an object from the class. 

**Concrete classes** are fully defined blueprints. You can use them to "instantiate" (create) objects directly in your game. 

**Abstract classes**, however, are considered "incomplete." They cannot be used to create objects. In our scenario, you wouldn't want a generic `Entity` object wandering around; you only want specific types like `Player` or `Enemy`.

highlights.text: concrete classes can be instantiated to create objects, whereas abstract classes cannot be used to create objects directly

steps.title: Comparison 2: Method Implementation
steps.content:
We also need to look at how methods are handled. 

In a **concrete class**, every single method must have a "body"—the actual code that tells the computer what to do. 

An **abstract class** can include **abstract methods**. These are essentially placeholders that define the method's signature (like `render()`) but contain no code. This is perfect for our game engine because while we know every entity must `render()`, the engine doesn't know *how* to draw a Dragon versus a Potion until the subclass defines it.

highlights.text: abstract classes can contain abstract methods (without bodies), while concrete classes must have implementations for all methods

steps.title: Benefit: Code Sharing
steps.content:
Why use an abstract class instead of just an interface or separate classes? 

It allows for **code sharing**. You can write the logic for `position` and `health` once in the abstract parent class so that `Player` and `Enemy` automatically inherit them. This follows the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle. At the same time, it protects the system by preventing the instantiation of a "generic" parent object that wouldn't have enough data to function correctly.

highlights.text: share common code and attributes among multiple subclasses while preventing the creation of the generic parent object

steps.title: Benefit: Enforcing a Contract
steps.content:
Finally, abstract classes are used to enforce a **contract** or structure. 

By declaring `update()` and `render()` as abstract methods in the parent class, the compiler forces any developer creating a new `Enemy` or `PowerUp` to provide an implementation for those methods. This ensures that the main Game Engine can safely call `.update()` on any entity, knowing for certain that the code exists.

highlights.text: enforce a specific structure (contract) on all inheriting subclasses through abstract method declarations

steps.title: Analyze Instantiation and Objects
steps.content:
In our GameEngine Pro scenario, a **concrete class** is one that we can actually use to create (instantiate) an object. For example, we want to create a specific `$Player$` or a specific `$Enemy$` to appear on the screen. 

In contrast, an **abstract class** like `Entity` cannot be used to create an object directly. You would never want a "generic entity" wandering around your game; it must be a specific type (like a Player) to function correctly.

steps.title: Compare Method Implementations
steps.content:
The next major difference is how they handle logic. An abstract class can contain **abstract methods**, which are declarations without any code (no "body"). 

In the scenario, `Entity` would define an abstract `update()` method. This acts as a placeholder. However, a **concrete class** like `Player` must provide the actual implementation (the code) for all its methods, including those it inherited from the abstract parent.

highlights.text: must implement their own update and render methods

steps.title: Understand Code Sharing Benefits
steps.content:
We use abstract classes when we want to share common code and attributes among multiple subclasses. 

By putting `$position$`, `$health$`, and `$collision detection$` into the abstract `Entity` class, we ensure all entities have these features without rewriting the code for every single one. This also prevents the creation of a "generic" parent object that doesn't have enough specific logic to exist on its own.

highlights.text: common behaviour for all game entities (position, health, collision detection)

steps.title: Enforce a Structural Contract
steps.content:
Finally, abstract classes are used to enforce a **contract** (a specific structure) on all subclasses. 

By declaring `update()` and `render()` as abstract in the `Entity` class, the GameEngine Pro framework guarantees that any developer creating a new entity (like a new `Boss` class) *must* include those methods. This ensures the engine's main loop can safely call `update()` on any object in the game world, knowing the method exists.

highlights.text: ensure that specific entity types (Player, Enemy, PowerUp) must implement their own update and render methods

parts.content: Using the GameEngine scenario, justify why an abstract `GameEntity` class would be beneficial for the game framework.
parts.markscheme:
- **Justify** that the `GameEntity` abstract class allows for the definition of common attributes (position, health) and shared methods (collision detection) once, reducing code redundancy <marks>A1</marks>
- **Justify** that declaring `update()` and `render()` as abstract methods forces subclasses like `Player`, `Enemy`, and `PowerUp` to implement their own specific logic while ensuring a consistent interface <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that it prevents the logical error of instantiating a generic `GameEntity` object, which would lack the necessary specific behaviors to function within the game <marks>A1</marks>

[3 marks total]

steps.title: Define the Abstract Blueprint
steps.content:
In IB Computer Science, an **abstract class** serves as a partial blueprint. It is used when we want to define a common identity for a group of objects but don't want anyone to create a "generic" version of that object. 

In this scenario, `GameEntity` acts as the base for all objects in the game world. This concept falls under **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**, specifically regarding abstraction and inheritance.

steps.title: Reduce Redundancy via Inheritance
steps.content:
One of the core justifications for using an abstract class is **inheritance**. By placing shared attributes like `position` and `health`, and shared methods like `collision detection` in the `GameEntity` class, we only have to write that code once.

This significantly **reduces code redundancy**. Instead of every `Player`, `Enemy`, or `PowerUp` having its own duplicate code for health or movement coordinates, they simply inherit these from the parent class.

steps.title: Template for Polymorphism
steps.content:
By declaring `update()` and `render()` as **abstract methods**, the abstract class provides a **template for polymorphism**. 

This "contract" forces every subclass to implement its own specific logic. For example:
- The `Player` might `update()` based on keyboard input.
- An `Enemy` might `update()` using AI logic.

Even though the logic is different, the **interface is consistent**. The game engine can simply loop through a list of entities and call `.update()` on all of them without needing to know exactly what type of object they are.

steps.title: Preventing Logical Errors
steps.content:
Finally, abstract classes **cannot be instantiated**. You can't write `new GameEntity()`.

This is beneficial because a generic entity doesn't have a specific shape or behavior; it wouldn't know how to move or what it looks like on screen. Preventing the creation of a generic `GameEntity` object ensures that only fully-functional objects (like a `Player` or `Enemy`) exist in the game world, avoiding potential logical crashes.

steps.title: Define the Shared Foundation
steps.content: From an architectural standpoint, the abstract `GameEntity` class serves as a master blueprint. By defining common attributes like **position** and **health**, and shared methods like **collision detection** in this single location, we ensure that every entity in the game follows the same data structure. This significantly reduces code redundancy and ensures that any change to the core entity logic only needs to be made once, maintaining a "single source of truth."

steps.title: Enforce an Architectural Contract
steps.content: The use of the `abstract` keyword for `update()` and `render()` methods acts as a mandatory **contract**. Because these methods have no implementation in the base class, the architecture *forces* any concrete subclass (like `Player` or `Enemy`) to provide its own specific logic. This guarantees that the game engine can reliably call `update()` on any object in the game world, knowing the object has definitely implemented that behavior, thus ensuring a consistent interface across the entire system.

steps.title: Ensure System Safety
steps.content: One of the most critical constraints of an abstract class is that it **cannot be instantiated**. In a game, a generic `GameEntity` is logically incomplete; it doesn't know what it looks like or how it behaves. By marking the class as `abstract`, we prevent the developer from accidentally creating a "naked" entity object that would cause the game to crash or behave unpredictably. This safety mechanism ensures that only fully defined objects like a `Player` or `PowerUp` can exist in the game memory.

steps.title: Final Justification
steps.content:
In summary, using an abstract class provides three key benefits:
1. **Efficiency**: Centralizes common code (position, health).
2. **Consistency**: Enforces a contract for specific behaviors (`update()`, `render()`). 
3. **Safety**: Prevents the instantiation of incomplete, generic objects.

This concept is a core part of **Topic B3.2.3: Abstraction in OOP**. Understanding how to constrain a system's design is essential for building scalable frameworks like game engines.

questions.specification: Case Study: MediaCenter Entertainment is building a media application where different player classes ($\text{AudioPlayer}$, $\text{VideoPlayer}$, $\text{StreamingPlayer}$) all need to support the same basic operations: $\text{play}()$, $\text{pause}()$, $\text{stop}()$, and $\text{getStatus}()$, but each implements these differently based on their media type.

parts.content: Define what is meant by "interfaces" in object-oriented programming.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that an interface is a contract or template that defines a set of method signatures that implementing classes must provide <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** that it specifies what methods a class must implement without providing the actual implementation details <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the Interface
steps.content: In object-oriented programming, an **interface** is formally defined as a **contract** or a **template**. This contract specifies a set of **method signatures** (the method names, return types, and parameters) that any class claiming to implement the interface must provide.

steps.title: Absence of Implementation
steps.content:
A key characteristic of an interface is that it provides **no implementation details**. It tells a class *what* it must do (e.g., it must have a $\text{play}()$ method), but it does not specify *how* that action is performed. 

In our case study, while $\text{AudioPlayer}$ and $\text{VideoPlayer}$ both use the same interface signatures, the actual code (logic) inside those methods will differ significantly based on the media type.

highlights.text: each implements these differently based on their media type

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, your definition should include these two distinct points:
1. **The Contract:** State that it is a contract/template defining method signatures for implementing classes.
2. **The Abstraction:** Explain that it specifies requirements without providing the actual implementation code.

parts.content: Compare interfaces with abstract classes in terms of their purpose and implementation constraints.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that interfaces define pure contracts with no method implementations (in most standard OOP languages) <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that classes can implement multiple interfaces, allowing for multiple inheritance of behavior <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** that abstract classes can provide both abstract methods and concrete (implemented) methods <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that classes can generally only inherit from one abstract class (single inheritance) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze Technical Constraints
steps.content:
To answer this question using a **Technical Constraint Comparison**, we need to look at the formal rules governing how interfaces and abstract classes are built. We specifically need to contrast how they handle method implementation and the rules for inheritance.

This topic falls under **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes (HL only)**. Understanding these constraints is vital for designing robust class hierarchies like the one for MediaCenter.

steps.title: Interfaces: The Pure Contract
steps.content:
The first technical constraint relates to method implementation. 

An **interface** serves as a "pure contract." In the standard OOP languages used in the IB curriculum, an interface cannot provide any actual code (implementation) for its methods. It only defines the method signatures, such as $\text{play}()$ and $\text{pause}()$. 

This forces any class, like $\text{AudioPlayer}$, to provide its own specific implementation for every single method listed in the interface.

highlights.text: interfaces define pure contracts with no method implementations

steps.title: Interfaces: Multiple Inheritance
steps.content:
The second constraint for interfaces involves inheritance limits. 

A single class can **implement multiple interfaces** simultaneously. This allows for "multiple inheritance of behavior." For example, a $\text{StreamingPlayer}$ could implement both an $\text{IPlayer}$ interface and an $\text{ICloudConnect}$ interface, satisfying two different sets of requirements at once.

highlights.text: classes can implement multiple interfaces, allowing for multiple inheritance of behavior

steps.title: Abstract Classes: Hybrid Methods
steps.content:
Now we compare this to **abstract classes**. 

A key technical difference is that an abstract class is not restricted to being a pure contract. It can contain:
1.  **Abstract methods**: Like an interface, these have no code and must be overridden.
2.  **Concrete methods**: These include full implementation (actual code). 

For MediaCenter, if $\text{getStatus}()$ is identical for all players, you could implement it once in the abstract class to save time and reduce code duplication.

highlights.text: abstract classes can provide both abstract methods and concrete (implemented) methods

steps.title: Abstract Classes: Single Inheritance
steps.content:
Finally, we look at the inheritance constraint for abstract classes. 

In most OOP environments, a class is limited by **single inheritance**. This means a class can generally only inherit from (extend) **one** abstract class. This is a stricter technical limit than interfaces, as it prevents a class from belonging to multiple distinct parent hierarchies.

highlights.text: classes can generally only inherit from one abstract class (single inheritance)

steps.title: Analyze the Architectural Roles
steps.content:
To compare interfaces and abstract classes, we look at their **architectural roles**. Think of an **interface** as a "pure contract"—a set of rules any class must follow. An **abstract class** acts as a "partial template"—it provides a base structure that related classes can share and build upon.

This distinction is crucial for the MediaCenter application, as different players might share some logic but must all adhere to a specific set of operations.

steps.title: Interface as a Pure Contract
steps.content:
An **interface** defines a pure contract. In standard Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), it contains only method signatures (like `play()` or `stop()`) without any code inside them. 

By using an interface, you ensure that any class—whether it's an `AudioPlayer` or a `StreamingPlayer`—guarantees it will provide its own specific implementation for those methods.

highlights.text: interfaces define pure contracts with no method implementations

steps.title: Multiple Inheritance of Behavior
steps.content:
A major implementation constraint (and benefit) of interfaces is that a single class can **implement multiple interfaces**. 

This allows for "multiple inheritance of behavior." For example, a `StreamingPlayer` could implement both a `Playable` interface and a `NetworkConnected` interface simultaneously.

highlights.text: classes can implement multiple interfaces, allowing for multiple inheritance of behavior

steps.title: Abstract Class as Template
steps.content:
An **abstract class** serves as a partial template. Unlike interfaces, it can contain both **abstract methods** (which have no code) and **concrete methods** (which have full implementations).

This is useful if all players in MediaCenter share common code (like a `volume` variable or a method to `logActivity()`). You write the code once in the abstract class, and all subclasses inherit it.

highlights.text: abstract classes can provide both abstract methods and concrete (implemented) methods

steps.title: Single Inheritance Constraint
steps.content:
The primary constraint for abstract classes is that a class can generally **only inherit from one abstract class**. 

This follows the principle of "single inheritance," which keeps the class hierarchy simple and avoids the complexities (like the "diamond problem") that occur when inheriting from multiple parents.

highlights.text: classes can generally only inherit from one abstract class (single inheritance)

parts.content: Using the MediaCenter case study, explain how an interface would solve this design challenge and describe the benefits.
parts.markscheme:
- **Outline** how a $\text{MediaPlayer}$ interface would define common method signatures such as $\text{play}()$, $\text{pause}()$, $\text{stop}()$, and $\text{getStatus}()$ <marks>A1</marks>
