- **Explain** that each player class (e.g., $\text{AudioPlayer}$, $\text{VideoPlayer}$) will implement this interface, ensuring consistent control behavior across different media types <marks>A1</marks>
- **Describe** a benefit such as polymorphism support, which allows the application to handle any player type through a common $\text{MediaPlayer}$ reference <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the Contractual Interface
steps.content:
In the **Contractual Interface Approach**, we start by defining a "contract" that all media players must follow. We create a $\text{MediaPlayer}$ interface that specifies the required method signatures: $\text{play}()$, $\text{pause}()$, $\text{stop}()$, and $\text{getStatus}()$.

This interface doesn't contain the actual code for how to play a file; instead, it acts as a formal agreement that any class claiming to be a "media player" must provide these specific functionalities. This is covered in **Topic B3.2.3: Explaining the Concept of Abstraction**.

steps.title: Implement Consistent Behavior
steps.content:
Next, each concrete class—such as $\text{AudioPlayer}$ and $\text{VideoPlayer}$—must "sign" the contract by implementing the interface. 

Even though an $\text{AudioPlayer}$ processes sound waves and a $\text{VideoPlayer}$ renders frames, they both provide their own version of the $\text{play}()$ method. This ensures **consistent control behavior** across the entire application, as every player is guaranteed to respond to the same set of commands.

steps.title: Leverage Polymorphic Benefits
steps.content:
The major benefit of this approach is **polymorphism**. Since every player follows the $\text{MediaPlayer}$ contract, the main application can interact with any object using a common $\text{MediaPlayer}$ reference.

For example, you could store all active players in a single list: 
$$\text{List}<\text{MediaPlayer}> \text{allPlayers}$$

You can then loop through this list and call $\text{.play}()$ on every item without the program needing to know the specific media type of each object. This makes the system highly extensible—if you add a $\text{StreamingPlayer}$ later, the core application logic doesn't need to change. This aligns with **Topic B3.2.2: Modeling Polymorphism**.

steps.title: Define the Media Interface
steps.content:
To solve this design challenge, we first create a $\text{MediaPlayer}$ interface. This interface acts as a formal contract that defines exactly which method signatures every player must have. 

In this case, the interface will declare the following common methods:
- $\text{play}()$
- $\text{pause}()$
- $\text{stop}()$
- $\text{getStatus}()$

By doing this, we establish a standard "blueprint" that any media-related class in the MediaCenter application must follow.

steps.title: Implement for Specific Types
steps.content:
Next, each specific player class—$\text{AudioPlayer}$, $\text{VideoPlayer}$, and $\text{StreamingPlayer}$—must **implement** the $\text{MediaPlayer}$ interface. 

While each class provides its own unique internal logic for how to actually "play" (e.g., a $\text{VideoPlayer}$ might need to initialize a screen while an $\text{AudioPlayer}$ only initializes speakers), they all use the exact same method names. This ensures consistent control behavior throughout the entire application.

steps.title: Apply Polymorphic Management
steps.content:
The greatest benefit of this design is **polymorphism**. Because all players implement the same interface, the application can store them in a single collection (like an array or list) of type $\text{MediaPlayer}$.

Instead of writing separate code to manage each type of player, you can use a single loop to control everything:

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{for each (MediaPlayer p in myPlaylist) \{} \\ &\quad p.\text{play}(); \\ &\text{\}} \end{aligned}$$

This "Polymorphic Management" makes the code much cleaner and allows MediaCenter to add new player types (like $\text{VRPlayer}$) in the future without changing the existing control logic. This concept is a key part of **Topic B3.2: Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes**.

questions.specification: Case Study: RoboticsLab is designing a Robot class that needs movement, sensing, and communication capabilities. They're debating between inheriting from a MovableRobot class versus composing the robot from separate MovementModule, SensorModule, and CommunicationModule objects.

parts.content: Define what is meant by "composition over inheritance" design principle.
parts.markscheme:
- A design principle that favors object composition over class inheritance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suggests building functionality by combining objects rather than inheriting from classes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the advantages of using composition instead of inheritance and describe scenarios where composition is preferable.
parts.markscheme:
- More flexible, can change behaviour at runtime <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Avoids inheritance hierarchy complexity and coupling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- When a "has-a" relationship is more natural than an "is-a" relationship <marks>1 mark</marks>
- When you need to combine features from multiple sources <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the RoboticsLab case study, compare how this could be implemented using inheritance versus composition and evaluate which approach would be more flexible.
parts.markscheme:
- Inheritance: Robot extends MovableRobot, which can limit future flexibility and create tight coupling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Composition: Robot contains MovementModule, SensorModule, CommunicationModule objects <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Composition is more flexible: modules can be swapped/changed independently (e.g., different sensors or movement systems) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported judgment: explicitly concludes which approach is more flexible for RoboticsLab with at least one relevant reason (e.g., easier extension, testing, maintenance) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Case Study: FinTechSolutions is developing a $PaymentProcessor$ class that needs to work with different payment methods ($CreditCardService$, $PayPalService$, $BankTransferService$). Currently, the class creates these service objects internally, making testing difficult and coupling it tightly to specific implementations.

Which statement best describes the object-oriented technique that improves testability by supplying a class’s required collaborating objects from outside the class instead of creating them internally?

options.content: Encapsulation, because it hides implementation details inside a class.
options.markscheme: Encapsulation is about restricting access to internal state/implementation; it does not specifically describe supplying collaborating objects from outside.

options.content: Inheritance, because it allows a class to reuse code from a parent class.
options.markscheme: Inheritance is about an is-a relationship and code reuse; it does not address providing dependencies externally for testability.

options.content: Dependency injection, because required services are provided to a class from an external source rather than created inside the class.
options.markscheme: Correct: dependency injection supplies a class’s dependencies (e.g., payment services) from outside, reducing coupling and improving testability.

options.content: Polymorphism, because the same method call can behave differently for different objects.
options.markscheme: Polymorphism enables substitutability via common interfaces, but the technique described (supplying dependencies externally instead of instantiating them internally) is dependency injection.

topic.title: B3.2 Fundamentals of OOP for multiple classes (HL only)
